name: William Thompson
summary: 68-year-old male with a history of COPD, incidentally found to have Stage IIIA Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (adenocarcinoma) during evaluation for a COPD exacerbation. Underwent concurrent chemoradiation, complicated by a pulmonary embolism requiring anticoagulation. Currently on maintenance therapy and anticoagulation.
storyline:
William Thompson, a 68-year-old retired mechanic with a significant smoking history and diagnosed COPD, initially presented to his primary care physician with a worsening COPD exacerbation. A chest X-ray revealed an unexpected lung opacity, prompting a subsequent CT scan which identified a suspicious mass in the right upper lobe. This led to a pulmonology referral, followed by bronchoscopy and biopsy, confirming Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer, adenocarcinoma subtype. Further staging with PET/CT and brain MRI revealed regional lymph node involvement, classifying it as Stage IIIA. 

He began concurrent chemoradiation therapy. During his second cycle of chemotherapy, he developed acute dyspnea and leg swelling, necessitating an emergency room visit where a CT pulmonary angiogram confirmed a pulmonary embolism. Anticoagulation with apixaban was promptly initiated, and his chemotherapy regimen was adjusted and continued carefully. He completed the full course of chemoradiation and is now on post-treatment surveillance, remaining on long-term anticoagulation for the PE. His case highlights the complexities of managing lung cancer in the context of significant comorbidities.

conditions.name: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)
conditions.notes: Long-standing history, managed with inhalers, presented with acute exacerbation.

conditions.name: Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer, Stage IIIA (Adenocarcinoma)
conditions.notes: Diagnosed via bronchoscopy biopsy, localized advanced disease.

conditions.name: Pulmonary Embolism (PE)
conditions.notes: Developed during chemotherapy treatment, likely due to malignancy and treatment-related hypercoagulability.

medications.name: Carboplatin
medications.treats: Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer, Stage IIIA (Adenocarcinoma)

medications.name: Pemetrexed
medications.treats: Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer, Stage IIIA (Adenocarcinoma)

medications.name: Ondansetron
medications.frequency: prior to chemo, then every 8 hours PRN
medications.notes: Anti-emetic for chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting.

medications.name: Apixaban
medications.notes: Anticoagulation for PE, initiated in ER.

visits.chief_complaint: Worsening shortness of breath, increased cough with sputum
visits.narrative:
Patient presents with a 5-day history of increased dyspnea, particularly with exertion, and a productive cough with yellow sputum, consistent with a COPD exacerbation. Reports using his rescue inhaler more frequently without significant relief. Vital signs: T 99.2 F, HR 98, RR 24, SpO2 90% on room air. Lungs with diffuse wheezes and prolonged expiration. 

Initiated oral prednisone 40mg daily for 5 days. Prescribed a 5-day course of Azithromycin. Ordered chest X-ray to rule out pneumonia. Advised to continue maintenance inhalers and follow up in 3 days for X-ray review and symptom reassessment.

visits.chief_complaint: COPD exacerbation follow-up, chest X-ray review
visits.narrative:
Patient returns for follow-up. Symptoms are marginally improved but still significant dyspnea. Chest X-ray shows a new, ill-defined opacity in the right upper lobe, not typical for an acute infection. No frank pneumonia. Given the patient's smoking history and age, further imaging is warranted.

Discontinued prednisone. Ordered a contrast-enhanced Chest CT to further characterize the right upper lobe opacity. Referral to pulmonology for evaluation of lung mass. Patient advised to complete antibiotic course and continue COPD management. Will contact with CT results and pulmonology appointment details.

procedures.name: Chest X-ray
procedures.outcome: New ill-defined opacity in right upper lobe.

referrals.reason: Evaluation of new right upper lobe lung mass on chest X-ray.

visits.chief_complaint: Chest CT results review, ongoing dyspnea
visits.narrative:
Patient reviewed today. Chest CT results show a 4.5 cm spiculated mass in the right upper lobe with associated hilar and mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Findings are highly suspicious for malignancy. 

Patient counseled on the findings and the high suspicion for lung cancer. Pulmonology appointment with Dr. James Miller has been scheduled for November 5th. Emphasized the importance of this consultation for further diagnostic workup. Continued supportive care for COPD.

procedures.name: Chest CT with contrast
procedures.outcome: 4.5 cm spiculated mass in RUL, hilar/mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

visits.narrative:
New patient consult for evaluation of right upper lobe lung mass. Reviewed Chest CT images. Discussed patient's smoking history and COPD. Physical exam unremarkable apart from diminished breath sounds at bases. 

Recommended diagnostic bronchoscopy with endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS) guided biopsy of the mass and mediastinal lymph nodes. Patient consented to the procedure. Discussed risks and benefits. Procedure scheduled for next week. Patient advised on pre-procedure fasting and medication adjustments.

Patient underwent bronchoscopy with EBUS-guided transbronchial needle aspiration (TBNA) of the right upper lobe mass and mediastinal lymph nodes (stations 4R and 7). Procedure tolerated well without complications. Tissue samples sent for histopathological analysis, immunohistochemistry, and molecular testing. 

Patient discharged home after recovery, stable. Advised to report any fever, hemoptysis, or increasing shortness of breath. Follow-up scheduled with pulmonology for results review.

procedures.name: Bronchoscopy with EBUS-guided biopsy
procedures.outcome: Samples obtained from RUL mass and mediastinal lymph nodes without complication.

visits.narrative:
Patient returned for review of biopsy results. Pathology confirms Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer, adenocarcinoma subtype, with positive lymph node involvement in station 4R. Molecular testing for EGFR, ALK, ROS1 pending. 

Patient counseled on the diagnosis of lung cancer. Explained the importance of further staging to guide treatment. Referral placed to oncology for comprehensive staging and treatment planning. Patient provided with resources for support. Oncologist consult scheduled.

referrals.reason: New diagnosis of NSCLC adenocarcinoma with positive lymph nodes for staging and treatment.

visits.chief_complaint: Oncology consult for newly diagnosed NSCLC
visits.narrative:
New patient oncology consult for adenocarcinoma of the lung. Reviewed outside records and biopsy results confirming NSCLC with regional lymph node involvement. Patient's performance status is good (ECOG 1). 

Ordered comprehensive staging workup: PET/CT scan to assess for distant metastases and brain MRI to rule out brain metastases. Discussed preliminary treatment options based on potential staging. Will review all imaging and molecular results at next visit to finalize a treatment plan. Patient given contact information for questions.

Patient seen for review of staging scans. PET/CT shows hypermetabolic activity only in the right upper lobe mass and associated hilar/mediastinal lymph nodes, no evidence of distant metastases. Brain MRI is negative for metastatic disease. Molecular testing showed no actionable mutations (EGFR/ALK/ROS1 negative).

Diagnosis confirmed as Stage IIIA Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (T2b N2 M0). Discussed multidisciplinary treatment approach including concurrent chemoradiation. Proposed regimen: Carboplatin + Pemetrexed every 3 weeks for 4 cycles, concurrently with thoracic radiation. Referral to Radiation Oncology for simulation and planning. Patient agreeable to treatment plan. Provided extensive education on treatment course and potential side effects.

procedures.name: PET/CT scan
procedures.outcome: Hypermetabolic RUL mass and regional lymph nodes, no distant mets.

referrals.reason: Concurrent radiation therapy planning for Stage IIIA NSCLC.

visits.narrative:
New patient radiation oncology consult. Reviewed CT and PET scans. Discussed concurrent chemoradiation plan for Stage IIIA NSCLC. Patient underwent radiation simulation today for treatment planning. Target volumes (GTV, CTV, PTV) delineated for IMRT. 

Discussed potential side effects, including esophagitis, pneumonitis, and fatigue. Consent obtained. Treatment expected to start next week, coordinated with medical oncology for chemotherapy cycles.

Patient initiated concurrent chemoradiation today. Received first cycle of Carboplatin (AUC 6) and Pemetrexed (500 mg/m²) intravenously. Pre-medications given including ondansetron. Tolerated infusion well with no immediate adverse reactions. 

Started daily radiation treatments today. Patient counseled again on common side effects such as nausea, fatigue, myelosuppression, and skin irritation. Labs (CBC, CMP) ordered prior to treatment and will be monitored weekly. Scheduled for next chemotherapy cycle in 3 weeks. Provided anti-emetics for home use.

visits.chief_complaint: Chemotherapy Cycle 2, new dyspnea and leg swelling
visits.narrative:
Patient presented for Cycle 2 of Carboplatin/Pemetrexed. Reports increasing dyspnea over the past 3 days, now persistent at rest, and new onset right leg swelling. Denies chest pain, cough, or fever. Radiation is ongoing.

Physical exam reveals mild tachypnea and unilateral (right) lower extremity edema, pitting to +2. Lungs with decreased breath sounds on the right. Given symptoms and hypercoagulable state of malignancy, high suspicion for pulmonary embolism or DVT. Chemotherapy held. Urgent labs ordered including D-dimer. Immediate referral to Emergency Department for further evaluation.

referrals.reason: Acute dyspnea and unilateral leg swelling, high suspicion for PE/DVT.

visits.chief_complaint: Acute dyspnea, right leg swelling, suspected PE
visits.narrative:
Patient presented to ED from oncology clinic with acute dyspnea and right leg swelling. Vitals: HR 110, RR 26, SpO2 88% on room air. D-dimer elevated at 1250 ng/mL FEU. CT Pulmonary Angiogram (CTPA) performed, confirming multiple bilateral pulmonary emboli, largest in the right main pulmonary artery. Right lower extremity ultrasound negative for DVT. 

Diagnosis: Acute Pulmonary Embolism. Initiated therapeutic anticoagulation with Apixaban 10mg BID for 7 days, then 5mg BID. Patient admitted for observation and cardiology consult. Oncology team notified.

Cardiology consult for newly diagnosed pulmonary embolism in a patient with active lung cancer. Reviewed CTPA and clinical status. Patient is hemodynamically stable. No evidence of right heart strain. 

Confirmed plan for long-term anticoagulation with Apixaban for at least 6 months, likely longer given active malignancy. Discussed risks of bleeding versus recurrence. Recommended close monitoring of CBC and renal function. Oncology team consulted for chemotherapy continuation strategy. Patient educated on signs/symptoms of bleeding and PE recurrence.

Patient presented for Cycle 3 of chemoradiation. PE diagnosed and successfully anticoagulated with Apixaban. Patient reports improved dyspnea and no further leg swelling. Labs today show improving counts: WBC 4.1, Hgb 11.5, Plt 130. Creatinine stable.

Reviewed with cardiology and radiation oncology. Agreed to proceed with Cycle 3 of Carboplatin/Pemetrexed, carefully monitoring for bleeding complications due to concurrent anticoagulation. Radiation therapy continuing without interruption. Patient tolerating Apixaban well. Emphasized adherence to both cancer treatment and anticoagulation.

Patient presented for Cycle 4 (final cycle) of Carboplatin/Pemetrexed. Overall feeling well. No new complaints. Radiation is nearing completion. Continues on Apixaban without any bleeding complications. 

Labs today within acceptable range for chemotherapy. Administered final cycle of systemic chemotherapy. Discussed plan for post-treatment evaluation scans approximately 4-6 weeks after completion of both chemotherapy and radiation. Patient remains engaged and motivated.

Patient completed 30 fractions of definitive radiation therapy for Stage IIIA NSCLC today. Tolerated treatment well with expected side effects of fatigue and mild esophagitis, which are resolving. Skin reaction minimal. 

Discussed acute and late radiation side effects. Patient instructed on supportive care measures. Follow-up with medical oncology for post-treatment surveillance scans. Provided contact for any questions or new symptoms.

Patient seen for post-treatment surveillance planning. Approximately 5 weeks since completion of chemoradiation. Reports improved energy levels, resolution of esophagitis, and stable respiratory status. Continues on Apixaban. 

Ordered repeat PET/CT scan to assess treatment response and rule out recurrence or progression. Also ordered a baseline CT chest without contrast for future surveillance. Labs (CBC, CMP) checked prior to imaging. Scheduled for follow-up in 1 week to review scan results.

visits.chief_complaint: Review of post-treatment PET/CT and surveillance plan
visits.narrative:
Patient returns for review of post-treatment PET/CT scan. Scan shows significant metabolic and size reduction of the right upper lobe mass and mediastinal lymph nodes, consistent with good partial response to therapy. No new hypermetabolic foci suggesting recurrence or metastases. 

Discussed findings. Plan for ongoing surveillance with CT chest without contrast every 3 months for the first year. Continue Apixaban indefinitely for cancer-associated thrombosis prophylaxis. Patient expressed relief and understanding of the surveillance plan. Next oncology follow-up in 3 months.

procedures.name: PET/CT scan (post-treatment)
procedures.outcome: Significant partial response of RUL mass and lymph nodes, no new disease.
